collector/benthos/internal/logging.purpose: Single-file bridge package that adapts Benthos' *service.Logger to a logr.LogSink, letting controller-runtime and klog route through the same Benthos logger instance. No business logic — purely a logging adapter.

patterns.name: Benthos-to-logr bridge via CtrlLogger
patterns.description: CtrlLogger implements logr.LogSink by wrapping *service.Logger. Info level 0 maps to Infof; any other level maps to Debugf; Error always calls Errorf. Construct via NewLogrLogger / logr.New(&CtrlLogger{logger: ...}).

patterns.name: SetupKlog called exactly once at startup
patterns.description: SetupKlog sets the global klog logger via klog.SetLogger with a logr.Logger carrying component=kubernetes. klog global state is not re-entrant, so call it once.

patterns.name: WithValues and WithName are intentional no-ops
patterns.description: Both methods return the same receiver unchanged. Benthos uses format strings, so structured kv pairs from controller-runtime are silently dropped by design — do not add state to them.

key_file_guides.role: Entire package: CtrlLogger struct (Init/Enabled/Info/Error/WithValues/WithName), NewLogrLogger factory, SetupKlog global initializer.
key_file_guides.watch_for: WithValues/WithName are no-ops — structured kv pairs are silently dropped; Enabled always returns true so level filtering must happen upstream.

anti_patterns: Adding a second logger abstraction — this package is purely a bridge, not a logging framework
Implementing WithValues/WithName with real state — the Benthos logger is format-string based and cannot carry structured fields
Calling SetupKlog more than once — klog global state is not re-entrant

decisions.decision: Map logr level 0 to Info and all other levels to Debug
decisions.rationale: controller-runtime uses level 0 for normal operational messages; higher levels are verbose debug output that should not appear at Info in production.
Distinguishes Kubernetes controller-runtime log lines from Benthos pipeline logs in aggregated log streams.

code_examples.scenario: Wire the Benthos logger into controller-runtime and klog

collector/benthos/internal/message.purpose: Provides the Batch type alias (= service.MessageBatch) and the Transaction primitive that associates a batch with its acknowledgement mechanism, preserving delivery guarantees as batches flow through pipeline stages.

patterns.name: Batch is a transparent type alias, not a newtype
patterns.description: type Batch = service.MessageBatch — all Benthos MessageBatch methods are available directly. Do not add methods to Batch; it must remain a transparent alias.

patterns.name: Transaction pairs payload with an ack mechanism
patterns.description: Transaction holds a Batch plus exactly one of responseChan (chan<-error) or responseFunc (func(ctx,err) error). Use NewTransaction for chan-based ack, NewTransactionFunc for func-based ack. Call Ack(ctx, nil) for success, Ack(ctx, err) to nack/retry upstream.
patterns.example: tx := message.NewTransactionFunc(batch, func(ctx context.Context, err error) error { return err }); return tx.Ack(context.Background(), nil)

patterns.name: WithContext for cancellation propagation
patterns.description: WithContext returns a shallow copy of the Transaction with a new ctx. Receivers should honour t.Context() cancellation but must STILL call Ack even when cancelled, passing t.Context().Err().

key_file_guides.watch_for: It is an alias (=), not a defined type — do not attach methods to Batch directly.

key_file_guides.role: Transaction struct with NewTransaction (chan-based) and NewTransactionFunc (func-based) constructors plus Context, WithContext, and Ack.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Exactly one of responseChan/responseFunc is non-nil; Ack branches on responseFunc != nil first. Ack is not idempotent — the chan send and responseFunc run again on repeat calls.

anti_patterns: Not calling Ack after processing — the upstream source stalls waiting for acknowledgement
Calling Ack multiple times on the same Transaction — the chan send and responseFunc are not idempotent
Ignoring t.Context() cancellation in a long reconnect loop — can block shutdown
Adding methods to Batch — it is a type alias and must remain a transparent pass-through

decisions.decision: Two ack paths: chan<-error vs func(context.Context, error) error
decisions.rationale: Chan-based ack is simpler for single-consumer paths; func-based ack supports async/contextual delivery confirmation needed by more complex output brokers.

decisions.decision: Transaction is its own concept rather than embedding service.Message
decisions.rationale: Batch expansion (splitting one batch into derived batches) breaks message-level ack; Transaction decouples ack from batch contents so derivative transactions can be tracked independently.

code_examples.scenario: Create and acknowledge a function-based transaction

collector/benthos/internal/shutdown.purpose: Provides the Signaller primitive for two-tier graceful shutdown (at-leisure then now) plus a HasClosed completion signal. Components own a Signaller; orchestrators call CloseAtLeisure/CloseNow; components call ShutdownComplete when done.

patterns.name: Two-tier shutdown: CloseAtLeisure then CloseNow
patterns.description: CloseAtLeisure closes closeAtLeisureChan (finish current work, start none new). CloseNow calls CloseAtLeisure first then closes closeNowChan (stop immediately). Each is guarded by sync.Once so concurrent calls are safe.
patterns.example:
sig.CloseAtLeisure() // soft stop
sig.CloseNow()       // hard stop — always calls CloseAtLeisure first

patterns.name: Chan-based and ctx-based wait helpers per tier
patterns.description: Each tier exposes *Chan() returning a read-only channel for select and *Ctx() wrapping it in a context. Use *Ctx() when combining shutdown with other cancellation sources.

patterns.name: ShutdownComplete signals component termination
patterns.description: The component calls sig.ShutdownComplete() once its goroutines have exited; orchestrators wait on HasClosedChan()/HasClosedCtx() to confirm safe teardown. Forgetting it blocks the orchestrator forever.

key_file_guides.role: Entire package: Signaller struct with CloseAtLeisure, CloseNow, ShutdownComplete and their *Chan/*Ctx/ShouldClose* accessors plus HasClosed.
key_file_guides.watch_for: CloseNow always calls CloseAtLeisure first — ShouldCloseAtLeisure is true whenever ShouldCloseNow is true; checking only the Now tier misses the leisure signal.

anti_patterns: Forgetting to call ShutdownComplete — orchestrators block forever on HasClosedChan
Adding a third shutdown tier — the two-tier model (leisure / now) is the intentional contract
Using CloseAtLeisureCtx when CloseNowCtx is needed — the leisure ctx fires on either tier; the now ctx fires only on the urgent tier
Closing the shutdown channels directly — always call CloseAtLeisure/CloseNow; a direct close panics on repeat calls

decisions.rationale: Closing an already-closed channel panics; Once makes CloseAtLeisure, CloseNow, and ShutdownComplete safe to call multiple times from concurrent goroutines.

decisions.decision: Separate Signaller per component rather than a global shutdown signal
decisions.rationale: Allows fine-grained teardown sequencing — the orchestrator can drain outputs before closing inputs by shutting down Signallers in dependency order.

code_examples.scenario: Component using Signaller for graceful shutdown
